authorship_evidence: Project created for EVO Summer Python Lab 2017, pre-LLM era. Single author (anxolerd) with consistent commit history and natural coding style.

evidence.description: SQL injection via Python string formatting. The 'name' parameter from user input is interpolated directly into the SQL query using Python's % operator: "VALUES ('%(name)s')" % {'name': name}. The resulting string is then passed to cur.execute(q) without parameterization. An attacker can submit a name like "Robert'); DROP TABLE students CASCADE; --" to execute arbitrary SQL. Documented in the project README.

Stored XSS via review text. The {{ review.review_text }} expression renders user-submitted review text without HTML escaping (autoescape is disabled globally in app.py). Any user can submit a review containing arbitrary HTML/JavaScript (e.g. <script>alert(document.cookie)</script>) via POST to /courses/{id}/review without authentication. The payload is stored in the database and rendered to every user who views the course page. Documented in the project README.

Stored XSS via student names. The {{ name }} expression in the students listing renders student names without HTML escaping. Student names are user-supplied via POST to /students/ (which has no backend authorization check). An attacker can create a student with a name containing JavaScript (e.g. <script>alert(1)</script>) and the payload will execute for every user viewing the students list page.

Stored XSS via course title and description. Lines 14-15 render {{ course.title }} and {{ course.description }} without HTML escaping. Course data is user-supplied via POST to /courses/ (backend has no admin authorization check despite the template gating the form). An attacker can create a course with XSS payloads in the title or description fields.

Stored XSS via course title and description in the courses listing page. Lines 17-18 render {{ course.title }} and {{ course.description }} without HTML escaping. Same root data as dvpwa-004 but a different rendering location affecting the courses index page.

Stored XSS via course title and description on the individual student page. Lines 19-20 render {{ course.title }} and {{ course.description }} without HTML escaping in the marks/results section. Same root data as dvpwa-004 but rendered on the student detail page.

Jinja2 template engine configured with autoescape=False. This disables automatic HTML escaping for all template variables, making every unescaped template expression a potential XSS sink. This is the root cause of all stored XSS vulnerabilities (dvpwa-002 through dvpwa-006). The secure default should be autoescape=True.

Passwords are hashed using unsalted MD5: md5(password.encode('utf-8')).hexdigest(). MD5 is cryptographically broken and unsuitable for password storage. The lack of salt means identical passwords produce identical hashes, enabling rainbow table attacks and statistical analysis. Passwords should use bcrypt, argon2, or PBKDF2. Documented in the project README.

CSRF protection is disabled. The csrf_middleware is commented out on line 27 (# csrf_middleware,) in the Application middlewares list. Although CSRF tokens are generated in templates, the middleware that validates them on POST requests is never active. This allows cross-site request forgery attacks against all state-changing endpoints. Documented as a vulnerability category in the project README.

Session cookie created with httponly=False. The RedisStorage is configured with httponly=False, meaning the AIOHTTP_SESSION cookie is accessible to JavaScript via document.cookie. Combined with the stored XSS vulnerabilities, this enables session hijacking. The cookie should have httponly=True to prevent JavaScript access.

The 500 error page renders {{ error.__dict__ }} in a <pre> tag, exposing the full internal error object dictionary to the client. This can leak stack traces, internal file paths, database query details, configuration values, and other sensitive server-side information to attackers.

Missing server-side authorization on student creation. The students view handles POST requests to create students without checking if the user is authenticated. The template (students.jinja2) only shows the form to authenticated users, but the backend performs no auth check. Any unauthenticated user can POST directly to /students/ to create student records.

Missing server-side authorization on course creation. The courses view handles POST requests to create courses without checking if the user is authenticated or is an admin. The template (courses.jinja2) only shows the form to admin users, but the backend performs no auth or admin check. Any unauthenticated user can POST directly to /courses/ to create course records.

Session fixation vulnerability. On successful login, the session ID is not rotated — the existing session simply has 'user_id' set (session['user_id'] = user.id) without creating a new session. An attacker who knows a victim's pre-authentication session ID (e.g. via the AIOHTTP_SESSION cookie) can hijack the authenticated session after the victim logs in. Documented in the project README.

Application runs with debug=True in the Application constructor. Debug mode can expose verbose error messages, stack traces, and internal application state to end users, aiding attackers in reconnaissance.

NOT VULNERABLE. Uses parameterized query with %s placeholder: cur.execute('...WHERE username = %s', (username,)). The %s is a psycopg2/aiopg parameter placeholder (not Python string formatting). The username value is passed as a separate tuple parameter, so the database driver handles proper escaping. This is the safe pattern for parameterized queries.

NOT VULNERABLE. Uses parameterized query with named placeholders: 'VALUES (%(course_id)s, %(review_text)s)'. The params dictionary {'course_id': course_id, 'review_text': review_text} is passed as a separate argument to cur.execute(q, params). The %(name)s syntax here is a psycopg2 named parameter placeholder, NOT Python string formatting — the key difference from the vulnerable Student.create which uses Python's % operator before passing to execute.

NOT VULNERABLE. Uses parameterized query with named placeholders: 'VALUES (%(title)s, %(description)s)'. The parameters are passed as a separate dictionary to cur.execute(q, {'title': title, 'description': description}). The %(name)s syntax is a psycopg2 named parameter placeholder passed separately to execute(), unlike the vulnerable Student.create which applies Python string formatting (% operator) before calling execute().

NOT VULNERABLE. The expression {{ student.name | e }} uses the Jinja2 escape filter (| e) to explicitly HTML-encode the student name before rendering. Despite autoescape=False being set globally, the explicit | e filter ensures that any HTML special characters in the student name are properly escaped. This stands in contrast to the same template's lines 19-20 which render course data without the escape filter.

Redis config schema and connection code have no password support. The CONFIG_SCHEMA does not include a password field and aioredis.create_pool() in sqli/services/redis.py does not pass one. This architecturally prevents Redis authentication even if desired, leaving session data accessible to anyone with network access to the Redis instance.

No rate limiting on the login endpoint. The index view handles authentication via POST with no throttling, lockout, or delay mechanism. Combined with weak MD5 password hashing (dvpwa-008), this enables practical online brute-force and credential stuffing attacks.

No rate limiting on non-authentication form submission endpoints. The POST handlers for students, courses, and reviews in views.py have no throttling or abuse prevention. While less critical than the login endpoint (dvpwa-017), this enables automated data creation, resource exhaustion, and potential abuse of the application.

Password hash exposed in template context via auth_user_processor. The function calls get_auth_user() which returns the full User NamedTuple (including pwd_hash) and passes it directly into every template's context as 'auth_user'. This makes the MD5 password hash accessible to all templates via auth_user.pwd_hash. Additionally, NamedTuple's default __repr__ includes all fields, so any logging of the User object would expose the hash and PII. While no current template renders pwd_hash and no log statement captures User objects, the sensitive data unnecessarily crosses from the auth layer into the rendering layer, violating the principle of least privilege.

Database DSN constructed via string formatting with plaintext password. The _init_pg function builds a DSN string by interpolating config values including the password: 'password={password}'.format(**conf). However, the DSN is a local variable used only during startup, is not logged by any code path, and is not exposed via error pages (startup errors occur before the HTTP server runs). Standard Python tracebacks show source lines, not interpolated variable values. The password already exists in cleartext in the config YAML and app['config']['db'] dict, making the DSN no more risky. Using keyword arguments to aiopg.create_pool() would be marginally better defensive practice.

No TLS/SSL configuration for PostgreSQL connection. The DSN does not include sslmode and no ssl argument is passed to aiopg.create_pool(). However, the deployment context significantly mitigates this: Docker Compose places postgres, redis, and the app on the same internal bridge network — traffic never leaves the host. No production config exists (only config/dev.yaml), the application is open-source and educational, and the database credentials are already postgres:postgres. Per severity guidance for insecure default settings in open-source local development features: Low.
